title: Rehab vs. Skilled Nursing: What's the Difference?
summary: These terms get used almost interchangeably, but they describe somewhat different things — and the difference affects both care and cost.
category: Life Transitions I: The Hospital-to-Home Arc
journeyStage: Life Transitions I: The Hospital-to-Home Arc
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Rehab vs. Skilled Nursing: What's the Difference? | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: These terms get used almost interchangeably, but they describe somewhat different things — and the difference affects both care and cost.
whatYouNeedToKnow: "Rehab" usually refers to short-term, goal-focused therapy after a hospitalization.
"Skilled nursing" can mean either short-term rehab or longer-term medical care.
The facility can be the same building, but the type of stay affects insurance coverage differently.

paragraphs: "Rehabilitation" typically describes a short-term stay focused on regaining specific functional abilities, often in the same building as a skilled nursing facility. "Skilled nursing" is a broader term that can describe either that same short-term rehab stay or longer-term care for ongoing medical needs — the distinction often depends on the specific goals and expected length of stay, not the building itself.

This distinction matters most for insurance: Medicare, for example, covers short-term skilled rehab differently than longer-term skilled nursing care, so it's worth asking directly which category applies.

nextSteps: Ask the discharge planner directly which category the stay falls under.
Confirm expected length of stay and what happens if goals aren't met by then.
Clarify coverage specifics with the facility's billing office early.
disclaimer: General terminology overview — a facility's admissions or billing staff can clarify the specific category and coverage for a given stay.

sources.name: Medicare.gov: Medicare Coverage of Skilled Nursing Facility Care
